narrative:
FIRST WARN FORECAST
Monday, June 8, 2026

A First Warn Weather Alert Day remains in effect as periods of showers and thunderstorms continue across Missouri and Illinois. While not everyone will see rain all day long, scattered rounds of thunderstorms are expected from morning through evening, and any storm that develops will have the potential to produce very heavy rainfall in a short amount of time.

The atmosphere remains loaded with moisture, and that means rainfall rates could be intense at times. Some locations may receive one to two inches of rain in less than an hour, creating localized flooding concerns. Areas that have already seen significant rainfall over the past several days will be especially vulnerable to flash flooding. Low-water crossings, poor drainage areas, urban streets, and small streams will be the most likely locations to experience flooding problems. Never drive through flooded roadways. It only takes a small amount of moving water to sweep a vehicle off the road.

As we move into the afternoon and early evening hours, thunderstorms are expected to become more numerous and stronger, especially along and east of the Mississippi River. Southeastern Missouri and southwestern Illinois will have the greatest opportunity to see severe weather. While widespread severe storms are not expected, a few storms could become organized enough to produce damaging wind gusts capable of downing tree limbs and causing isolated power outages. In addition, atmospheric wind patterns near the ground will support a low but non-zero tornado threat. Any tornado that develops would likely be brief and localized, but residents should remain weather aware and have multiple ways to receive warnings.

The severe weather and flooding threats will gradually diminish after sunset as daytime heating fades and the storm system responsible for our unsettled weather finally begins to weaken and move away. Showers and a few thunderstorms may linger into the evening, but overall coverage and intensity should slowly decrease overnight.

Once today's storm system exits, the weather pattern will undergo a dramatic change. A strong ridge of high pressure will build across the central United States, ushering in the hottest stretch of weather so far this year. Temperatures will surge into the lower and middle 90s Tuesday through Thursday, while dew points climb well into the 70s. The combination of heat and humidity will make it feel significantly hotter than the actual air temperature.

Heat index values are expected to reach between 100 and 105 degrees Tuesday and Wednesday across much of the region. These conditions can quickly lead to heat-related illnesses for anyone spending extended periods outdoors. Be sure to stay hydrated, take frequent breaks if working outside, and never leave children or pets unattended in vehicles. Heat advisories may be issued as confidence continues to increase in this dangerous early-season heat event.

The next opportunity for widespread thunderstorms arrives late Thursday into Thursday night as a cold front approaches from the northwest. Depending on the timing of that front, some storms could become strong or severe. Behind the front, temperatures may ease slightly heading into next weekend, although the pattern remains somewhat unsettled with occasional chances for showers and thunderstorms.

For today, keep the umbrella nearby, stay alert for changing weather conditions, and monitor forecasts throughout the day as heavy rain, localized flooding, and a few severe storms remain possible.

First. Accurate. Trusted.

First Warn Weather
Serving Missouri and Illinois
